mnemonics.title: BASIS Remember
mnemonics.content: B - Basis vectors A - Are S - Spanning I - Independent S - Sets. Remember: A good BASIS is both SPANNING and INDEPENDENT.

mnemonics.title: Change of Basis Flow
mnemonics.content: To change coordinates: Identify bases → Build transition matrix P → Find P⁻¹ → Multiply. P⁻¹ goes 'against' P: B'→B uses P, B→B' uses P⁻¹.

mnemonics.title: Coordinate Vector
mnemonics.content: Coordinates = Coefficients in linear combination. Think: v = c₁b₁ + c₂b₂ + ... → [v]ᵇ = (c₁, c₂, ..., cₙ). The 'c' stands for coefficient!

mnemonics.title: Unique Coordinates
mnemonics.content: U - Unique N - Numbers I - In every C - Coordinate system. Just like your fingerprint is unique to you, coordinates are unique to each vector in a given basis.

acronyms.usage: Converts coordinates from one basis to another. P has new basis vectors as columns in old basis coordinates.
B = {b₁, b₂, ..., bₙ} - the reference for expressing coordinates. Order matters!

acronyms.expansion: Coordinate vector of v relative to basis B
acronyms.usage: Read as 'v in basis B'. Column vector showing coefficients of basis vectors.
Linear transformation from vector space V to Rⁿ mapping each vector to its coordinate vector.

visualTips.visualization: Think of coordinates as 'shadows' or projections of a vector onto each basis direction. In standard basis, coordinates are exactly the perpendicular projections onto x, y, z axes.
Changing basis is like rotating your viewpoint. The vector itself doesn't change, only how you describe it. Like describing a point using different coordinate systems.

A basis defines your coordinate system. Standard basis = rectangular axes. Other bases = skewed or transformed axes. The vector stays the same; coordinates tell you 'how much' of each basis direction.
The transition matrix acts as a translator between languages (bases). P translates from B' language to B language; P⁻¹ translates back from B to B'.

analogies.analogy: Just as GPS coordinates (latitude, longitude) describe a location relative to Earth's reference points, vector coordinates describe a vector relative to basis vectors. Change the reference points (basis), and the coordinates change even if the location (vector) stays the same.
Like translating a sentence from English to French. The meaning (vector) stays the same, but the representation (coordinates) changes. The transition matrix is like a translation dictionary.
The coordinate mapping is like a converter that turns a vector into its 'address' in terms of basis building blocks. Each coordinate tells you how many of that building block you need.

Dimension is like the number of ingredients needed to make any dish in a cuisine. In 3D space, you need 3 independent directions (ingredients); in 2D, just 2. The basis tells you exactly which ingredients (vectors) to use.
